title: Dispatch Reports & Statistics
lede: Know exactly how your field business is doing — revenue, profit, technician payouts, and close rates — without touching a spreadsheet. This guide walks the three reporting views inside Dispatch: the Statistics dashboard, the Finance Report, and the Team Member Report.

steps.title: Find the three reporting views in Dispatch
steps.content:
Every job your team runs generates money data — and iMash turns it into three ready-made reports, each answering a different question:

  • Statistics — "How is the business doing?" KPI cards and charts for the owner's daily glance.
  • Finance Reports — "Where did every dollar go?" A job-by-job financial ledger with exports.
  • Team Reports — "Who do I owe, and who owes me?" Technician settlement, payroll, and commissions.
  1. In the top navigation bar, click Dispatch. You land on the Statistics view — the section's home.
  2. Look at the colorful chip strip right below the navigation bar. Scroll it sideways to find the Statistics, Finance Reports, and Team Reports chips — those are the three views this guide covers.
💡 Each view is deep-linkable: the address bar updates as you switch chips, so you can bookmark your favorite report and jump straight to it every morning.

images.alt: The Dispatch section open on the Statistics view with the chip strip visible
images.caption: Dispatch home: the Statistics view, with the chip strip on top — Finance Reports and Team Reports live a few chips to the right.

images.alt: The Dispatch section open on the Statistics view with the chip strip visible
images.caption: Dispatch home: the Statistics view, with the chip strip on top — Finance Reports and Team Reports live a few chips to the right.

steps.title: Statistics: set the period and read the KPI cards
steps.content:
The Statistics view opens with a date toolbar and a row of KPI cards. Set the period first — everything on the page recomputes instantly:

  1. Pick a preset from the first dropdown — Today, Yesterday, This Week (Mon–Today), Last Week (Mon–Sun), Last 7 days, This month, Last month, Last 30 days — or click the date range field to set exact Custom range dates.
  2. Check Filter By. It defaults to Completion Date, which means revenue is counted in the period the work was finished — the number a business owner actually wants. Click the small arrow to reveal Added Date, Scheduled Start, and Scheduled End if you prefer a different basis.
  3. Click the refresh icon any time to recompute with fresh data.

The KPI cards then tell the story:

  • Jobs — how many jobs fall in the period.
  • Total — the actual amount charged on paid invoices (including tax, net of refunds). Tips are excluded on purpose.
  • Tips — customer tips collected in the period. Tips go 100% to the technician, so they get their own card instead of inflating revenue.
  • Company's Cut — your share after technician commissions.
  • Cancelled and Completed — counts plus each one's percentage of all resolved jobs, so the two always add up to 100%. This is your close rate.
  • Calls — calls linked to dispatch activity in the period.

images.alt: The Statistics KPI cards: Jobs, Total, Tips, Company's Cut, Cancelled, Completed, and Calls
images.caption: The Statistics KPI row — Jobs, Total, Tips, Company's Cut, Cancelled, Completed, and Calls.

images.alt: The Statistics KPI cards: Jobs, Total, Tips, Company's Cut, Cancelled, Completed, and Calls
images.caption: The Statistics KPI row — Jobs, Total, Tips, Company's Cut, Cancelled, Completed, and Calls.

steps.title: Statistics: explore the leaderboards and breakdown charts
steps.content:
Below the KPI cards, six chart cards break the same period down from every angle:

  • Top Team Members — a revenue leaderboard for your technicians. Each row shows the tech's revenue, a green/red completion bar with their completion percentage, a completed/total job count, and their average sale per job. Hover the bar for the exact numbers.
  • Top Performance By — the same leaderboard idea, but grouped by Job Source or Job Type. Use the small dropdown in the card title to switch between the two.
  • Tickets by Status — a donut of every job in the period by its current status (scheduled, dispatched, completed, cancelled, and so on), with the total in the corner.
  • Jobs by Area — where your work is coming from geographically, grouped by city/area.
  • Job Sources by Sales Amount — which lead sources actually turn into money. Each legend row shows the dollars, the total vs. completed job counts, and the average sale for that source.
  • Job Sources by Done Jobs — the same sources ranked purely by completed-job volume.
💡 Use Job Sources by Sales Amount when deciding where to spend marketing budget — a source that sends lots of jobs but few dollars will be obvious at a glance.

images.alt: The Tickets by Status donut chart with a per-status legend
images.caption: Tickets by Status: every job in the period, split by its current status.

images.alt: The Tickets by Status donut chart with a per-status legend
images.caption: Tickets by Status: every job in the period, split by its current status.

steps.title: Statistics: track a year of earnings in one chart
steps.content:
At the bottom of Statistics sits the Earnings Breakdown (Last 12 Months) card — your year at a glance, from completed jobs only:

  • Total — everything charged to customers each month (including tax and card surcharge, excluding tips).
  • Company's Cut — the company's share, shown with its percentage of Total.
  • Tech's Cut — what your technicians earned, also as a percentage of Total.
  • Team Cut — commissions paid to additional team members (helpers, salespeople, managers on a cut). This chip appears only once you actually pay team commissions.

The summary chips on top give the 12-month grand totals; the grouped columns below show the same split month by month, so seasonality and margin trends jump out immediately. Hover any column for the exact dollar amounts.

images.alt: The Earnings Breakdown card with Total, Company's Cut and Tech's Cut summary chips above a monthly column chart
images.caption: Earnings Breakdown: 12-month totals on top, the month-by-month split below.

images.alt: The Earnings Breakdown card with Total, Company's Cut and Tech's Cut summary chips above a monthly column chart
images.caption: Earnings Breakdown: 12-month totals on top, the month-by-month split below.

steps.title: Open the Finance Report — your job-by-job ledger
steps.content:
When you need the receipts behind the pretty charts, the Finance Report is a full financial ledger — one row per job, every dollar accounted for:

  1. In the Dispatch chip strip, click Finance Reports.
  2. The table lists every job in the selected period. Swipe or scroll it horizontally — the columns run: #, Ticket (click the job number to open the job), Address, Job Source, Team Member, Job Type, Start Date, Scheduled, Closed, then the money columns — Total, Tips, Company Parts, Tech Parts, Company Total, Tech Total, Fees, and a column per payment method.
💡 Every Ticket number is a link — spot a weird amount, click it, and you're inside that job's Financials tab in one click.

images.alt: The Finance Reports view with the date toolbar, money tiles and the jobs ledger table
images.caption: Finance Reports: toolbar on top, money tiles in the middle, and the job-by-job ledger below.

images.alt: The Finance Reports view with the date toolbar, money tiles and the jobs ledger table
images.caption: Finance Reports: toolbar on top, money tiles in the middle, and the job-by-job ledger below.

steps.title: Finance Report: dates, date basis, and one-click exports
steps.content:
The Finance Report toolbar works like the Statistics one, with two extras — exports:

  1. Pick a date preset or a Custom range, exactly as in Statistics.
  2. Choose the Filter By basis. The Finance Report defaults to Added Date (the day the job was created); click the arrow to switch to Completion Date, Scheduled Start, or Scheduled End.
  3. Click CSV to download the ledger as a spreadsheet, or PDF for a polished, branded report you can hand to an accountant or partner.
  4. Click the refresh icon to re-run the report after changing anything.
⚠️ Date basis matters. A job added in June but completed in July counts in June under Added Date and in July under Completion Date. Pick one basis and stick to it when comparing periods, or your month-over-month numbers won't line up.

images.alt: The Finance Report toolbar with the date preset, range picker, Filter By buttons, CSV and PDF export buttons
images.caption: The Finance Report toolbar: presets, exact dates, the Filter By basis, and CSV / PDF exports.

images.alt: The Finance Report toolbar with the date preset, range picker, Filter By buttons, CSV and PDF export buttons
images.caption: The Finance Report toolbar: presets, exact dates, the Filter By basis, and CSV / PDF exports.

steps.title: Finance Report: read the money tiles
steps.content:
Between the toolbar and the table, a scrollable strip of tiles totals the filtered jobs. Hover any tile for a plain-language explanation of exactly what's inside the number:

  • Total (charged, incl. tax) — the real amount charged on paid invoices: including tax, net of refunds, tips excluded. On multi-invoice jobs, every invoice is counted.
  • Tips — customer tips (100% to technicians). Kept out of Total so revenue stays honest.
  • Tax — sales tax collected (a government pass-through, already inside Total).
  • Company Gross / Technician Gross — how the work value splits between the company and the techs, before parts and fees.
  • Jobs — the row count behind all these totals.
  • Company Total / Tech Total — each side's take-home after parts and commissions.
  • Technician Cut — total commissions earned by technicians.
  • Company Parts — parts cost the company covered.
  • Fees — card/processing costs actually paid to the payment processor.
  • Per-payment-method tiles — Cash, Credit, Card, Check, Zelle, Venmo and friends, each totaling what came in through that method. Methods with zero activity hide automatically.

images.alt: The Finance Report money tiles: Total, Tips, Tax, Company Gross, Technician Gross and Jobs
images.caption: The first tiles in the strip — scroll right for take-home totals, parts, fees, and per-payment-method tiles.

images.alt: The Finance Report money tiles: Total, Tips, Tax, Company Gross, Technician Gross and Jobs
images.caption: The first tiles in the strip — scroll right for take-home totals, parts, fees, and per-payment-method tiles.

steps.title: Finance Report: slice the ledger with filters
steps.content:
Want the numbers for one lead source, one tech, or one type of work? The filter drawer slices the whole report:

  1. Click the blue filter (funnel) button at the right end of the toolbar. The Report Filters drawer opens.
  2. Fill in any combination of: Job Sources (one or more lead sources), Team Members (one or more technicians), Job Types, and Job Status (Draft, Scheduled, Dispatched, In Progress, On Hold, Completed, Cancelled, Spam).
  3. Click Apply Filters. Every tile and every table row recomputes to the filtered set — and the CSV / PDF exports honor the filters too.
💡 Month-end routine worth stealing: filter to one Job Source, set the month, and export the PDF — that's a ready-made statement for your lead-source partner.

images.alt: The Report Filters drawer with Job Sources, Team Members, Job Types and Job Status fields
images.caption: The Finance Report filter drawer — combine any filters, then click Apply Filters.

images.alt: The Report Filters drawer with Job Sources, Team Members, Job Types and Job Status fields
images.caption: The Finance Report filter drawer — combine any filters, then click Apply Filters.

steps.title: Open the Team Report — settlement and payroll central
steps.content:
The Team Report answers the question every field-service owner asks on payday: "who owes whom, and how much?"

  1. In the Dispatch chip strip, click Team Reports.
  2. The toolbar on top mirrors the Finance Report — date presets, a custom range, the Filter By basis (here it defaults to Completion Date, since you settle on finished work), CSV and PDF exports, and the filter drawer button.
  3. Below it: a strip of settlement tiles, then the Team Member Report card with its three views (next steps).

images.alt: The Team Reports view with the date toolbar, settlement tiles and the Team Member Report card
images.caption: Team Reports: toolbar, settlement tiles, and the Team Member Report card with its View By switcher.

images.alt: The Team Reports view with the date toolbar, settlement tiles and the Team Member Report card
images.caption: Team Reports: toolbar, settlement tiles, and the Team Member Report card with its View By switcher.

steps.title: Team Report: understand the settlement tiles
steps.content:
The tile strip is the settlement summary — scroll it sideways, and hover any tile for its exact definition:

  • Balance — the headline number. A red We owe tech badge means the company owes the technicians; a green Tech owes us badge means techs collected cash and owe the company; Settled means you're even.
  • Completed Jobs / Canceled Jobs — the period's job outcome counts.
  • Avg Ticket — the average sale per completed job, with the customer's grand total in parentheses.
  • Grand Total — everything customers actually paid: work + tax + tips + card fees.
  • Job Gross — the sale for the work only (no tax, tips, or fees) — the cleanest "how much did we sell" number.
  • Tips, Tax, Processing Fees — the pass-through amounts, separated out.
  • Company Gross / Company Profit and Technician Cut / Profit / Gross / Parts — each side of the split, before and after parts.
  • Company Parts and Card Cost — the company's parts spend and what the card processor actually charged you.
  • Payment totals show — a small control tile with Tax/Tip/Fee toggles that switch what every per-payment-method tile (Cash, Credit, Card, Check…) includes. Toggle a part on or off and all payment tiles recompute.

images.alt: The Team Report settlement tiles: Balance, Completed Jobs, Canceled Jobs, Avg Ticket, Grand Total and Job Gross
images.caption: The start of the settlement strip — Balance first, then the money breakdown; scroll right for the full set.

images.alt: The Team Report settlement tiles: Balance, Completed Jobs, Canceled Jobs, Avg Ticket, Grand Total and Job Gross
images.caption: The start of the settlement strip — Balance first, then the money breakdown; scroll right for the full set.

steps.title: Team Report: settle jobs in the Jobs Balance List
steps.content:
The Team Member Report card has a View By switcher with three views. The first, Jobs Balance List, is the working view — one row per job with a checkbox, the client info, dates, status, the money split (Total, Payment, Company Parts, Technician Parts), and the two columns that matter on payday: Settlement (who owes whom on this job) and Balance. A bold totals row is pinned to the top of the table.

  1. Tick the checkboxes of the jobs you're settling with a technician.
  2. Click Mark Paid when you've paid out (or collected) — the rows flip to paid and drop out of the open balance. Made a mistake? Select them again and click Mark Unpaid.
  3. Click Email Reports to generate a personal PDF statement per technician from the selected rows and email it to them — no more screenshots in group chats.
  4. The green Results badge on the right always shows how many rows the current filters produced.
⚠️ The Team Report only lists jobs that have a team member assigned — a job with no technician can't owe anyone anything. If the list looks empty, check your date basis (Completion Date is the default) and that the jobs in the period actually have techs assigned.

images.alt: The Team Member Report card in the Jobs Balance List view with Mark Paid, Mark Unpaid and Email Reports buttons
images.caption: Jobs Balance List: the View By switcher, the Mark Paid / Mark Unpaid settlement buttons, and Email Reports.

images.alt: The Team Member Report card in the Jobs Balance List view with Mark Paid, Mark Unpaid and Email Reports buttons
images.caption: Jobs Balance List: the View By switcher, the Mark Paid / Mark Unpaid settlement buttons, and Email Reports.

steps.title: Team Report: Summary and Team Commissions views
steps.content:
The other two View By options roll the same data up per person:

  • Summary — one row per team member with their Email, their net Balance for the period, and an Action column. Select members here and Email Reports sends each one their own statement.
  • Team Commissions — commissions earned by additional team members beyond the assigned technician: helpers, salespeople, or managers on a recurring cut. Each row shows the member (with their role tag), how many Jobs contributed, the Total, and the Unpaid / Paid split, with actions to mark commissions paid. Expand a row to see the per-job breakdown — the job, the assigned tech, the commission Rule that generated it, the Amount, and its Status.
💡 Team members earn commissions in two ways: a Recurring Commission rule in the member's settings (Rates & Fees), or a one-off addition on a specific job's Financials tab. If the Team Commissions view is empty, that's where to set them up.

images.alt: The Team Member Report card switched to the Summary view
images.caption: Summary view: one balance row per team member, ready for Email Reports.

images.alt: The Team Member Report card switched to the Summary view
images.caption: Summary view: one balance row per team member, ready for Email Reports.

images.alt: The Team Member Report card switched to the Team Commissions view
images.caption: Team Commissions view: extra team members' earnings, with the built-in hint on how commissions are configured.

images.alt: The Team Member Report card switched to the Team Commissions view
images.caption: Team Commissions view: extra team members' earnings, with the built-in hint on how commissions are configured.

steps.title: Team Report: filter to the jobs you're settling
steps.content:
The Team Report's filter drawer is tuned for payroll runs:

  1. Click the blue filter (funnel) button in the toolbar to open Report Filters.
  2. Set any of: Team Members (settle one tech at a time), Job Status (defaults to Completed — you normally only settle finished work), Payment Status (All, Paid, or Unpaid — pick Unpaid to see only what's still open), and Payment Type (for example only cash jobs, where the tech is holding your money).
  3. Click Apply Filters.
💡 The classic payday combo: one Team Member + Payment Status Unpaid + this week. What's left on screen is exactly the settlement conversation — then select all, Mark Paid, and Email Reports.

images.alt: The Team Report filter drawer with Team Members, Job Status, Payment Status and Payment Type fields
images.caption: The Team Report filters — note Job Status defaults to Completed.

images.alt: The Team Report filter drawer with Team Members, Job Status, Payment Status and Payment Type fields
images.caption: The Team Report filters — note Job Status defaults to Completed.

steps.title: Which report do I open? (cheat sheet)
steps.content:
All three views read from the same job data — they just answer different questions. Bookmark the one that matches yours:

  • "How did we do this week?" → Statistics — KPIs, close rate, leaderboards, source performance.
  • "I need the numbers for my accountant / a lead-source partner." → Finance Reports — filter, then CSV or PDF.
  • "It's payday — what do I owe my techs?" → Team Reports — Balance tile, Jobs Balance List, Mark Paid, Email Reports.

One thing to keep straight across all three: the Filter By date basis. Statistics and Team Reports default to Completion Date; the Finance Report defaults to Added Date. If two views ever "disagree," this is almost always why — switch them to the same basis and the numbers reconcile.

That's it — you now have a live P&L, a full ledger, and a payroll desk built into Dispatch. No spreadsheet required.

nextSteps.title: What's Next?
nextSteps.description: The reports are only as good as the data flowing into them — set up your jobs, team, and sources so every dollar lands in the right bucket.

cards.title: Job Sources, Types & Payment Types
cards.description: Clean source/type/payment lists make the breakdowns in these reports meaningful.

support.title: Need Help?
support.content: Numbers not adding up, or a report not showing what you expect? Email support@imash.io and the iMash team will help you reconcile it.

metaDescription: Master iMash's three Dispatch reporting views: the Statistics dashboard, the job-by-job Finance Report with CSV/PDF exports, and the Team Member Report for technician settlement, payroll, and team commissions.
